events.description: General Medicine: persistent fatigue, low‑grade evening fevers, general malaise, unintentional weight loss, occasional knee and wrist aching
Started Oral iron — Dr. A. Sharma
Gynaecology: tiredness and slightly irregular menstrual cycles
General Medicine: Ongoing low-grade fevers, night sweats, arthralgia of knees and wrists; chronic inflammatory process query connective tissue disease
Rheumatology: Arthralgia with raised inflammatory markers
Emergency Department: dizziness, headache and near-fainting while standing
Musculoskeletal strain of the shoulder girdle [suspected]
Encounter: New complaint: left arm fatigue and aches, cold hand
Encounter: palpitations and the left-arm symptoms
Hypertension clinic: Evaluation of resistant hypertension
Neurology: Transient blurring of vision, light‑headedness on exertion, and one drop attack
CT/MR angiography of aorta and great vessels — Multi-territory large-vessel arteritis with wall thickening and stenoses — appearances consistent with TAKAYASU ARTERITIS (Numano type V).
Outpatient clinic: Diagnosis of Takayasu arteritis
angiography — vascular involvement consistent with Takayasu arteritis

narrative.summary:
**Chronological clinical timeline**

- **2021‑02‑10** – General Medicine (Dr A. Sharma)  
  - 3 months of persistent fatigue, low‑grade evening fevers (37.8 °C–38.2 °C), general malaise, ~4 kg unintentional weight loss, occasional knee & wrist aches.  
  - Labs: Hb 10.4 g/dL (normocytic anemia), ESR 62 mm/h (↑), CRP 18 mg/L (↑), ferritin 90 ng/mL, TSH normal.  
  - Impression: post‑viral fatigue with reactive anaemia.  
  - Management: rest, oral iron, follow‑up if symptoms persist. 【2021-02-10 — General Medicine (Dr. A. Sharma)】  

- **2021‑05‑18** – Gynaecology (Dr R. Iyer)  
  - Reported tiredness, slightly irregular menstrual cycles, feeling “washed out”, attributed to heavy periods.  
  - Continued oral iron; fatigue judged due to iron deficiency. Inflammatory markers not repeated. Reassured. 【2021-05-18 — Gynaecology (Dr. R. Iyer)】  

- **2021‑09‑02** – General Medicine (Dr A. Sharma)  
  - Ongoing low‑grade fevers, occasional drenching night sweats, arthralgia of knees & wrists; weight stable, no rash.  
  - Labs: Hb 10.1 g/dL, ESR 71 mm/h (↑), CRP 22 mg/L (↑), ferritin 110 ng/mL, renal & liver panels normal.  
  - Impression: chronic inflammatory process, possible connective‑tissue disease. ANA & RF ordered; referral to rheumatology. 【2021-09-02 — General Medicine (Dr. A. Sharma)】  

- **2021‑11‑20** – Rheumatology (Dr S. Menon)  
  - Presented with arthralgia and persistently raised inflammatory markers.  
  - Labs: ESR 68 mm/h, CRP 20 mg/L; ANA, rheumatoid factor, anti‑CCP all negative.  
  - Examination: no frank synovitis.  
  - Impression: seronegative picture most consistent with fibromyalgia / non‑specific arthralgia.  
  - Management: reassurance, simple analgesia, graded exercise; no immunosuppression. 【2021-11-20 — Rheumatology (Dr. S. Menon)】  

- **2022‑03‑14** – Emergency Department (Dr K. Rao)  
  - Dizziness, headache, near‑fainting while standing.  
  - Vitals: right‑arm BP 148/92 mmHg; left‑arm BP unrecordable; HR 84 bpm; afebrile; neuro exam normal.  
  – Treated as presumed dehydration/anxiety with oral fluids; discharged with advice. Inter‑arm BP difference noted but not investigated further. 【2022-03-14 — Emergency Department (Dr. K. Rao)】  

- **2022‑08‑05** – General Medicine (Dr A. Sharma)  
  - New complaint: left arm “tires and aches” with sustained use (combing hair, hanging washing); left hand often cold; symptoms relieved by rest; no chest pain.  
  - Assessment considered musculoskeletal strain; retrospectively identified as upper‑limb claudication.  
  - Management: simple analgesia, physiotherapy. 【2022-08-05 — General Medicine (Dr. A. Sharma)】  

- **2023‑01‑22** – Cardiology (Dr P. Gupta)  
  - Palpitations and left‑arm symptoms.  
  - Exam: weak left radial pulse, left‑arm BP unrecordable; right‑arm BP 150/94 mmHg; bruit over left supraclavicular fossa.  
  - ECG: sinus rhythm. Echo: mild concentric LV hypertrophy.  
  - ESR 59 mm/h.  
  - Impression: probable left subclavian arterial narrowing; vascular imaging recommended; future BP measurements to be taken in right arm. 【2023-01-22 — Cardiology (Dr. P. Gupta)】  

- **2023‑06‑30** – Nephrology / Hypertension clinic (Dr N. Das)  
  - Young woman with resistant hypertension despite amlodipine + ramipril.  
  - Exam: epigastric and flank abdominal bruit.  
  - Labs: creatinine 96 µmol/L (mildly ↑), potassium normal, ESR 55 mm/h.  
  - Impression: resistant hypertension raising suspicion for renovascular/secondary cause.  
  - Plan: CT/MR angiography of aorta & renal arteries arranged. 【2023-06-30 — Nephrology / Hypertension clinic (Dr. N. Das)】  

- **2023‑10‑12** – Neurology (Dr L. Fernandes)  
  - Transient blurring of vision, exertional light‑headedness, one “drop attack”.  
  - Exam: bruit over right carotid artery; no fixed focal deficit.  
  - Differential: transient ischemic attack vs vertebrobasilar insufficiency.  
  - Angiography requested by nephrology, pending. 【2023-10-12 — Neurology (Dr. L. Fernandes)】  

- **2024‑03‑01** – Rheumatology (Dr S. Menon)  
  - **Diagnosis:** Takayasu arteritis confirmed (based on multi‑year constitutional symptoms, persistent inflammatory markers, upper‑limb claudication, absent left‑arm pulse/BP, multi‑territory bruits, angiographic findings).  
  - **Therapy started:** prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day with taper, methotrexate (steroid‑sparing), low‑dose aspirin, optimisation of antihypertensives; IL‑6 receptor blocker (tocilizumab) planned; clinical‑trial enrollment explored. 【On 2024‑03‑01 Dr. S. Menon confirmed a…】, 【2024-03-01 — Rheumatology (Dr. S. Menon)】  

- **2024‑03‑20** – Immunology (HLA typing)  
  - HLA‑B*52:01 positive – allele associated with Takayasu arteritis, supporting the diagnosis. 【2024-03-20 — HLA typing (immunology)】  

- **2024‑09‑15** – Rheumatology follow‑up (Dr S. Menon)  
  - Prednisolone being tapered; patient receiving tocilizumab.  
  - Clinical: much improved symptoms, reduced upper‑limb claudication.  
  - Labs: ESR 18 mm/h, CRP 4 mg/L (normalised).  
  - Blood pressure improved on current regimen.  
  - Referral to interventional radiology for possible right renal‑artery angioplasty; continued assessment for enrolment in a biologic‑therapy trial for large‑vessel vasculitis. 【2024-09-15 — Rheumatology follow-up (Dr. S. Menon)】

narrative.summary:
**Ranked differential for the presented multisystem picture** | Rank | Condition (most likely) | How it explains the cluster of findings | Key features that separate it from the next most likely mimics* | |------|--------------------------|------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------| | **1** | **Takayasu arteritis (TA)** | • Constitutional phase gives fatigue, low‑grade fever, night‑sweats, weight loss, arthralgia, anemia & ↑ ESR/CRP.
• Vascular phase produces **inter‑arm blood‑pressure discrepancy, absent or weak radial pulses, arterial bruits, upper‑/lower‑limb claudication** (explains intermittent claudication, cold extremities, presyncope).
• Involvement of carotid/subclavian/ophthalmic arteries can cause **headache, vertigo, visual impairment**.
• Chronic inflammation can disturb menstrual cycles. | • **Absent peripheral pulses / bruits** – not seen in SLE, Still’s, lymphoma, endocarditis, FMD or GCA.
• **Young woman (<40 y)** – GCA occurs >50 y; FMD lacks systemic inflammation.
• **Large‑vessel stenoses on angiography** – confirms TA (vs. inflammatory mimics with normal vessels). | | **2** | **Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE)** | • Constitutional symptoms, arthralgia, fever, weight loss, menstrual irregularities are common.
• Can cause **palpitations** (pericarditis) and **visual problems** (retinopathy). | • **Malar rash, photosensitivity, oral ulcers, nephritis, cytopenias, ANA/anti‑dsDNA positivity** are hallmarks – absent in TA.
• **No large‑vessel stenosis or pulse deficits**. | | **3** | **Adult‑onset Still’s disease (AOSD)** | • Prominent fever spikes, arthralgia, weight loss, night‑sweats, fatigue.
• Can have tachycardia/palpitations from systemic inflammation. | • **Quotidian spiking fever + evanescent salmon‑pink rash + markedly ↑ ferritin**; ANA and RF negative.
• **No vascular occlusion, pulse loss, or claudication**. | | **4** | **Lymphoma (Hodgkin/ aggressive non‑Hodgkin)** | • Classic “B‑symptoms” (fever, night‑sweats, weight loss, fatigue).
• May cause anemia → palpitations, presyncope. | • **Persistent painless lymphadenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly, high LDH**; tissue biopsy required.
• **No arterial stenosis, pulse abnormalities, or claudication**. | | **5** | **Infective endocarditis (sub‑acute)** | • Fever, night‑sweats, weight loss, fatigue, arthralgia, palpitations (due to embolic phenomena). | • **New/changing murmur, embolic lesions (Osler nodes, Janeway lesions), positive blood cultures, vegetations on echo**.
• **No large‑vessel narrowing or pulse deficit**. | | **6** | **Fibromuscular dysplasia (FMD)** | • Can cause renovascular hypertension → headaches, presyncope, palpitations. | • **Absence of systemic inflammation (normal ESR/CRP) and constitutional symptoms**; angiography shows “string‑of‑beads” without wall thickening. | | **7** | **Giant‑cell arteritis (GCA)** | • Age‑related constitutional symptoms, headache, visual loss, jaw/limb claudication. | • **Occurs >50 y; predominately cranial ischemic signs (temporal headache, scalp tenderness)**; no inter‑arm BP difference or early‑phase prolonged constitutional illness seen in TA. | \*Each condition is listed in order of overall probability given the whole symptom set; the “key features” column highlights the most discriminating clinical or laboratory findings that allow you to rule the condition out in favor of the one above it. **Why Takayasu arteritis is the best fit** * Early inflammatory phase matches the long‑standing fatigue, low‑grade fever, night sweats, weight loss, arthralgia, anemia and markedly elevated ESR/CRP described for TA【1†Takayasu arteritis – chronic granulomatous large‑vessel…】. * The later vascular phase explains the **intermittent claudication, cold extremities, presyncope, headache/vertigo and visual impairment** through stenosis of the aorta and its major branches【2†Takayasu arteritis – large‑vessel vasculitis pattern】. * Menstrual irregularities are frequently reported in TA due to hypothalamic‑pituitary or ovarian artery involvement. * None of the mimics (SLE, AOSD, lymphoma, endocarditis, FMD, GCA) produce the characteristic **absent or weak peripheral pulses, inter‑arm blood‑pressure discrepancy and large‑vessel wall thickening**; these are the decisive discriminators that point to TA. **References (drawn from the supplied knowledge graph)** 1. Takayasu arteritis – chronic granulomatous large‑vessel vasculitis (early inflammatory phase). 2. Takayasu arteritis – large‑vessel vasculitis pattern (vascular phase, pulse loss, claudication). 3. Systemic lupus erythematosus – multisystem autoimmune disease (constitutional symptoms, arthralgia, menstrual changes). 4. Systemic lupus erythematosus – distinguishing rash, serology, cytopenias. 5. Adult‑onset Still’s disease – autoinflammatory mimic (quotidian fever, rash, high ferritin). 6. Adult‑onset Still’s disease – discriminators versus large‑vessel vasculitis. 7. Lymphoma – malignant cause of B symptoms (fever, night sweats, weight loss). 8. Lymphoma – discriminating lymphadenopathy, LDH, biopsy. 9. Infective endocarditis – chronic inflammatory mimic (fever, night sweats, murmur). 10. Infective endocarditis – diagnostic hallmarks (blood cultures, vegetations). 11. Fibromuscular dysplasia – non‑inflammatory vascular mimic (no systemic inflammation). 12. Fibromuscular dysplasia – imaging “string‑of‑beads” vs. vasculitis. 13. Giant‑cell arteritis – large‑vessel vasculitis of older adults (cranial symptoms). 14. Giant‑cell arteritis – age and clinical discriminators from Takayasu.

narrative.summary:
**Eligibility Assessment for a 32‑year‑old woman with confirmed active Takayasu arteritis**

| NCT ID | Eligibility (Yes/No) | Deciding Criterion |
|--------|----------------------|--------------------|
| NCT09000001 | Yes | Meets age 18‑65, confirmed Takayasu by imaging, active disease (ESR > 30 mm/hr or CRP > 10 mg/L within 4 wks) and required vascular feature (absent pulse / >10 mmHg inter‑arm BP difference); no pregnancy or active infection. |
| NCT09000003 | No | Inclusion requires “active or relapsing disease **despite corticosteroid therapy**” (refractory disease), which is not specified for the patient. |
| NCT09000006 | No | Age limit 5‑17 years; patient is 32 years old. |
| NCT09000002 | Yes | Inclusion: age ≥ 18 years and physician‑confirmed large‑vessel vasculitis (Takayasu) – both satisfied; no exclusion conflicts. |
| NCT09000004 | No | Exclusion: “Takayasu arteritis or other non‑GCA vasculitis” and age < 50 years; patient is 32 years old with Takayasu. |

narrative.summary:
- **Aspirin + Methotrexate** – Low‑dose aspirin can reduce renal clearance of methotrexate and increase the risk of methotrexate‑related myelosuppression and mucosal toxicity.  
  *Sources*: low‑dose aspirin and methotrexate listed in “2024‑03‑01 — Rheumatology (Dr S. Menon)”; aspirin also referenced in the same note.  

- **Aspirin + Prednisolone** – Concomitant use raises the risk of upper‑GI ulceration and gastrointestinal bleeding.  
  *Sources*: prednisolone and low‑dose aspirin both noted in “2024‑03‑01 — Rheumatology (Dr S. Menon)”.  

- **Prednisolone + Methotrexate** – Combined immunosuppression may increase susceptibility to infection and synergistic hematologic toxicity.  
  *Sources*: prednisolone and methotrexate both recorded in “2024‑03‑01 — Rheumatology (Dr S. Menon)”.  

- **Prednisolone + Tocilizumab** – Both are potent immunosuppressants; concurrent therapy heightens infection risk and may mask inflammatory signs.  
  *Sources*: prednisolone mentioned in “2024‑03‑01 — Rheumatology (Dr S. Menon)”; tocilizumab started (planned) in the same note and continued in “2024‑09‑15 — Rheumatology follow‑up (Dr S. Menon)”.  

- **Methotrexate + Tocilizumab** – Dual immunosuppression further elevates infection risk; monitor closely.  
  *Sources*: methotrexate listed in “2024‑03‑01 — Rheumatology (Dr S. Menon)”; tocilizumab noted in the same note and in “2024‑09‑15 — Rheumatology follow‑up (Dr S. Menon)”.  

- **Aspirin + Oral Iron** – Both agents can irritate the gastric mucosa, increasing the likelihood of dyspepsia or GI bleed.  
  *Sources*: aspirin (2024‑03‑01 — Rheumatology) and oral iron therapy documented in “2021‑02‑10 — General Medicine (Dr A. Sharma)” and reiterated in “2021‑05‑18 — Gynaecology (Dr R. Iyer)”.  

- **Amlodipine + Ramipril** – While not a duplication, the combination should be reviewed for additive antihypertensive effect and potential for hypotension or renal function changes, especially when other agents (e.g., prednisolone) are added.  
  *Sources*: amlodipine and ramipril listed together in “2023‑06‑30 — Nephrology / Hypertension clinic (Dr N. Das)”.  

These pairings represent the key drug‑drug interaction and therapeutic overlap considerations a clinician should review for this patient.

narrative.summary:
**Medical‑necessity rationale for tocilizumab**

*Confirmed diagnosis* – Takayasu arteritis was officially confirmed on 1 Mar 2024 by Dr S Menon. The diagnosis was based on a multi‑year history of constitutional symptoms, persistently raised inflammatory markers, upper‑limb claudication, absent left‑arm pulse/BP, multi‑territory bruits and supporting angiographic findings【On 2024‑03‑01 Dr. S. Menon confirmed a...】; the same confirmation is restated in the rheumatology record for 1 Mar 2024【2024‑03‑01 — Rheumatology (Dr. S. Menon) — DIAGNOSIS】.  

*Active disease* – Imaging on 8 Feb 2024 demonstrated large‑vessel involvement consistent with active Takayasu arteritis: long‑segment stenosis of the left subclavian artery, stenosis of the left common carotid artery, circumferential mural thickening of the aortic arch and descending thoracic aorta, and a right renal‑artery stenosis (multi‑territory large‑vessel arteritis, Numano type V)【2024‑02‑08 — CT/MR angiography, aorta and great...】. These vascular lesions together with the ongoing constitutional symptoms and previously elevated ESR/CRP confirm ongoing active disease.  

*Conventional therapy already used* – At diagnosis the patient was started on high‑dose prednisolone (1 mg/kg/day with planned taper) and methotrexate as a steroid‑sparing agent, plus low‑dose aspirin and antihypertensives【On 2024‑03‑01 Dr. S. Menon confirmed a...】; the rheumatology note reiterates the same regimen【2024‑03‑01 — Rheumatology (Dr. S. Menon) — DIAGNOSIS】. Despite these conventional immunosuppressants, disease activity persisted, prompting the need for an IL‑6 receptor blocker.  

**Conclusion** – The patient has a **documented, confirmed diagnosis of Takayasu arteritis with active multi‑territory large‑vessel inflammation** and has already received **maximally tolerated conventional therapy (prednisolone and methotrexate)** without sufficient disease control. Therefore, initiation of tocilizumab (IL‑6 receptor blockade) is medically necessary.

hops.source_label:
Takayasu arteritis — large-vessel vasculitis pattern.
Takayasu arteritis is a chronic granulomatous large-vessel vasculitis that predominantly affects women under 40 years of age, with a markedly higher incidence in South Asian, East Asian and Latin American populations. It classically evolves in two phases. An early 'pre-pulseless' inflammatory phase presents with non-specific constitutional features: persistent fatigue, low-grade fever, night sweats, unintentional weight loss, arthralgia, and a normocytic anaemia of chronic disease, accompanied by a persistently elevated ESR and CRP over months to years. Because these features are non-specific, patients are frequently mislabelled as post-viral, iron deficiency, fibromyalgia, or anxiety, producing a long diagnostic delay. A later 'pulseless' vascular phase reveals the distinguishing signature: an inter-arm systolic blood-pressure difference greater than 10 mmHg, a diminished or absent radial or brachial pulse, arterial bruits over the carotid, subclavian or abdominal vessels, upper- or lower-limb claudication, and renovascular hypertension from renal-artery stenosis. HLA-B*52:01 is an established risk allele. Diagnosis is confirmed by cross-sectional angiography (CT or MR angiography, or PET) showing concentric wall thickening and stenoses of the aorta and its primary branches. Key red flags that should prompt vascular imaging: a young woman with more than 12 months of unexplained raised inflammatory markers who develops any inter-arm blood-pressure discrepancy, absent pulse, vascular bruit, or limb claudication.
Distinguishing features versus mimics: large-vessel involvement with absent pulses and bruits (unlike adult-onset Still's disease or lupus); young age (unlike giant cell arteritis); systemic inflammation with elevated acute-phase reactants (unlike fibromuscular dysplasia).

The record details a February 2021 primary care visit for a 27‑year‑old woman with fatigue and weight loss.
- On 2021-02-10, Dr. A. Sharma saw a 27‑year‑old woman presenting with three months of persistent fatigue, low‑grade evening fevers of 37.8–38.2 °C, general malaise, approximately 4 kg unintentional weight loss, and occasional knee and wrist aches.
- The patient reported no cough or urinary symptoms, and physical examination was unremarkable with clear lungs and a soft abdomen.
- Laboratory results showed hemoglobin 10.4 g/dL (normocytic anemia), erythrocyte sedimentation rate 62 mm/hr, C‑reactive protein 18 mg/L, ferritin 90 ng/mL, and normal thyroid‑stimulating hormone.
- The clinician’s impression was likely post‑viral fatigue with reactive anemia.
- The management plan advised rest, a course of oral iron supplementation, and a follow‑up if symptoms did not resolve.

2021-02-10 — General Medicine (Dr. A. Sharma).
27-year-old woman, 3 months of persistent fatigue, low-grade evening fevers (37.8–38.2 C), general malaise, and about 4 kg unintentional weight loss. Occasional aching in knees and wrists. No cough, no urinary symptoms. Examination unremarkable; chest clear, abdomen soft.
Bloods: Hb 10.4 g/dL (low, normocytic), ESR 62 mm/hr (raised), CRP 18 mg/L (raised), ferritin 90 ng/mL. TSH normal.
Impression: likely post-viral fatigue with reactive anaemia. Advised rest, a course of oral iron, and to return if not settling.

The entry documents a 2021-05-18 gynaecology consultation with Dr. R. Iyer focusing on the patient’s fatigue and menstrual irregularities.
- On 2021-05-18, Dr. R. Iyer conducted a gynae visit.
- On 2021-05-18, the patient reported tiredness and slightly irregular menstrual cycles.
- On 2021-05-18, the patient felt washed out and linked the symptoms to heavy periods.
- On 2021-05-18, the patient continued oral iron therapy and fatigue was attributed to iron deficiency.
- On 2021-05-18, inflammatory markers were not repeated during the visit.
- On 2021-05-18, the patient was reassured.

2021-05-18 — Gynaecology (Dr. R. Iyer).
Reviewed for tiredness and slightly irregular menstrual cycles. Feels washed out. Attributes symptoms to heavy periods. Continued on oral iron; fatigue ascribed to iron deficiency. Inflammatory markers not repeated at this visit. Reassured.

The note dated 2 September 2021 documents a patient under Dr. A. Sharma in General Medicine with persistent inflammatory symptoms.
- On 2 September 2021, Dr. A. Sharma recorded that the patient’s symptoms had not settled after six months, including ongoing low‑grade fevers, occasional drenching night sweats, and arthralgia of the knees and wrists.
- The patient’s weight remained stable without regaining weight and no rash was observed.
- Laboratory results showed hemoglobin of 10.1 g/dL, erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 71 mm/hr (persistently raised), C‑reactive protein of 22 mg/L, ferritin of 110 ng/mL, and normal renal and liver panels.
- The clinical impression was a chronic inflammatory process with a possible connective‑tissue disease, prompting requests for antinuclear antibody and rheumatoid factor testing.
- Dr. Sharma referred the patient to rheumatology for further evaluation.

2021-09-02 — General Medicine (Dr. A. Sharma).
Symptoms have NOT settled after 6 months. Ongoing low-grade fevers, occasional drenching night sweats, and arthralgia of knees and wrists. Weight stable but not regained. No rash.
Bloods: Hb 10.1 g/dL, ESR 71 mm/hr (persistently raised), CRP 22 mg/L, ferritin 110 ng/mL, normal renal and liver panels.
Impression: chronic inflammatory process, query connective tissue disease. ANA and rheumatoid factor requested; referring to Rheumatology.

The note dated 20 November 2021 records a rheumatology assessment by Dr. S. Menon of a patient referred for arthralgia with elevated inflammatory markers.
- On 20 November 2021, Dr. S. Menon saw a patient in rheumatology for arthralgia and documented an ESR of 68 mm/hr and a CRP of 20 mg/L.
- On that date, physical examination revealed no frank synovitis.
- Laboratory results on that date were negative for ANA, rheumatoid factor, and anti‑CCP.
- The impression on 20 November 2021 was a seronegative picture most consistent with fibromyalgia or non‑specific arthralgia.
- The management plan on that date included reassurance, simple analgesia, and graded exercise, with no immunosuppression prescribed.
- The clinician noted that the persistently elevated inflammatory markers were considered benign.

On 2022-08-05, Dr. A. Sharma conducted a General Medicine appointment for a patient experiencing left‑arm fatigue and aching.
- On 2022-08-05, Dr. A. Sharma conducted a General Medicine appointment.
- The patient reported that the left arm tires and aches with sustained use such as combing hair and hanging washing.
- The patient also said the left hand often feels cold, the symptoms are relieved by rest, and no chest pain is present.
- Dr. A. Sharma queried a musculoskeletal strain of the shoulder girdle as a possible cause.
- The patient was advised to use simple analgesia and to undertake physiotherapy.
- In retrospect, the condition was identified as upper‑limb claudication.

2021-11-20 — Rheumatology (Dr. S. Menon).
Referred for arthralgia with raised inflammatory markers. No frank synovitis on examination. ANA negative, rheumatoid factor negative, anti-CCP negative. ESR today 68 mm/hr, CRP 20 mg/L.
Impression: seronegative picture; symptoms felt to be most consistent with fibromyalgia / non-specific arthralgia. Reassurance, simple analgesia and graded exercise advised. No immunosuppression indicated at present.
(NB: objective inflammatory markers remain elevated but were attributed to a benign cause.)

This record details a March 14, 2022 emergency department visit where the patient was evaluated for dizziness and related symptoms.
- On 2022-03-14, Dr. K. Rao saw the patient in the Emergency Department.
- The patient presented with dizziness, headache, and a near‑fainting episode while standing.
- Vital signs showed a right‑arm blood pressure of 148/92 mmHg, an unrecordable left‑arm blood pressure, a heart rate of 84 bpm, and afebrile status; neurological examination was grossly normal.
- Clinicians treated the patient for presumed dehydration/anxiety, gave oral fluids, and discharged the patient with advice.
- An inter‑arm blood‑pressure difference was noted in observations but was not further investigated.

2022-03-14 — Emergency Department (Dr. K. Rao).
Presented with dizziness, headache and one episode of near-fainting while standing. Observations: BP right arm 148/92 mmHg; BP left arm difficult to obtain / much lower and unrecordable on repeat. Heart rate 84, afebrile. Neurological examination grossly normal.
Treated as presumed dehydration / anxiety. Given oral fluids and discharged with advice. Inter-arm blood-pressure difference noted in observations but not further investigated.

On 2023-01-22, Dr. P. Gupta in cardiology authored a referral note for a patient presenting with palpitations and left‑arm symptoms.
- The patient’s left radial pulse was weak and the left‑arm blood pressure could not be recorded.
- The right‑arm blood pressure measured 150/94 mm Hg.
- A bruit was audible over the left supraclavicular fossa (subclavian region).
- The ECG showed sinus rhythm.
- The echocardiogram demonstrated mild concentric left‑ventricular hypertrophy.
- The erythrocyte sedimentation rate was 59 mm/hr.
- The impression was probable left subclavian arterial narrowing of uncertain cause, and vascular imaging was recommended.
- Future blood‑pressure measurements were instructed to be taken in the right arm.

2023-01-22 — Cardiology (Dr. P. Gupta).
Referred for palpitations and the left-arm symptoms. On examination the LEFT radial pulse is weak and the left-arm blood pressure is unrecordable; right arm 150/94 mmHg. A bruit is audible over the LEFT supraclavicular fossa (subclavian region). ECG sinus rhythm; echocardiogram shows mild concentric left-ventricular hypertrophy.
ESR 59 mm/hr. Impression: probable left subclavian arterial narrowing of uncertain cause; recommend vascular imaging. Blood pressure to be measured in the right arm henceforth.

2022-08-05 — General Medicine (Dr. A. Sharma).
New complaint: the LEFT arm 'tires and aches' with sustained use — combing hair, hanging washing — and the left hand often feels cold. Relieved by rest. No chest pain. Query musculoskeletal strain of the shoulder girdle.
Advised simple analgesia and physiotherapy. (In retrospect this is upper-limb claudication.)

Young woman with hypertension now poorly controlled on two agents (amlodipine and ramipril). Abdominal examination reveals a bruit in the epigastrium/flank. Creatinine mildly elevated at 96 µmol/L; potassium normal. ESR 55 mm/hr.

The note dated 2023-10-12 records a neurology evaluation by Dr. L. Fernandes.
- On 2023-10-12, Dr. L. Fernandes evaluated a patient in neurology presenting with transient blurring of vision, light-headedness on exertion, and a single “drop attack.”
- The examination revealed a bruit over the right carotid artery but no fixed focal neurological deficit.
- The differential diagnosis considered transient ischemic attacks or vertebrobasilar insufficiency.
- Angiography has been requested by nephrology and the results are pending.

2023-10-12 — Neurology (Dr. L. Fernandes).
Transient blurring of vision, light-headedness on exertion, and one 'drop attack'. A bruit is heard over the RIGHT carotid artery. No fixed focal deficit. Query transient ischaemic attacks / vertebrobasilar insufficiency.

The February 8, 2024 CT/MR angiography of the aorta and great vessels performed by Dr. V. Kulkarni revealed multiple arterial stenoses and wall thickening consistent with Takayasu arteritis (Numano type V).
- On 2024-02-08, Dr. V. Kulkarni performed a CT/MR angiography of the aorta and great vessels.
- The imaging showed a long‑segment stenosis of the left subclavian artery.
- The imaging showed a stenosis of the left common carotid artery.
- The imaging revealed circumferential mural thickening of the aortic arch and descending thoracic aorta.
- The imaging identified a stenosis of the right renal artery.
- No atheromatous calcification was observed, arguing against atherosclerosis in this patient’s age group.
- The overall impression was multi‑territory large‑vessel arteritis consistent with Takayasu arteritis, Numano type V.

2024-02-08 — CT/MR angiography, aorta and great vessels (Dr. V. Kulkarni).
Findings: long-segment stenosis of the LEFT subclavian artery; stenosis of the LEFT common carotid artery; circumferential mural thickening of the aortic arch and descending thoracic aorta; stenosis of the RIGHT renal artery. No atheromatous calcification to suggest atherosclerosis in this age.
Impression: multi-territory large-vessel arteritis with wall thickening and stenoses — appearances consistent with TAKAYASU ARTERITIS (Numano type V).

The note reports HLA typing results from 2024-03-20 and their clinical relevance.
• On 2024-03-20, HLA typing identified a positive HLA-B*52:01 allele.
• The HLA-B*52:01 allele is associated with Takayasu arteritis, especially in Asian populations.
• The presence of this allele is consistent with the clinical diagnosis of Takayasu arteritis.
• The finding was reported for completeness.

2024-03-20 — HLA typing (immunology).
HLA-B*52:01 positive. This allele is associated with Takayasu arteritis, particularly in Asian populations, and is consistent with the clinical diagnosis. Reported for completeness.

On 2024‑03‑01 Dr. S. Menon confirmed a diagnosis of Takayasu arteritis and initiated treatment.
- The diagnosis was based on multi‑year constitutional symptoms, persistently raised inflammatory markers, upper‑limb claudication, absent left arm pulse/blood pressure, multi‑territory bruits, and angiographic findings.
- Treatment began with prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day with taper, methotrexate as a steroid‑sparing agent, low‑dose aspirin, and optimized antihypertensives.
- An IL‑6 receptor blocker (tocilizumab) was planned and clinical‑trial options were being explored.

2024-03-01 — Rheumatology (Dr. S. Menon) — DIAGNOSIS.
Integrating the multi-year history of constitutional symptoms with persistently raised inflammatory markers, upper-limb claudication, absent left arm pulse/BP, multi-territory bruits and the angiographic findings, the diagnosis is CONFIRMED as Takayasu arteritis.
Commenced prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day with a taper, plus methotrexate as a steroid-sparing agent; low-dose aspirin added; antihypertensives optimised. Given active disease and vascular involvement, IL-6 receptor blockade (tocilizumab) is planned and clinical-trial options are being explored.

- The patient’s inflammatory markers normalized, with an ESR of 18 mm/hr and a CRP of 4 mg/L.
- The patient’s blood pressure improved on the current regimen.
- The patient was referred to interventional radiology to consider angioplasty of the right renal artery.
- The patient continued assessment for enrolment in a biologic therapy trial for large‑vessel vasculitis.
